Q1i.scenario: EV Insurance is a medium-sized life and health insurance company. Its major products are Individual Income Protection (IP) and Private Medical Insurance (PMI). EV Insurance has recently started selling impaired life annuities but has stopped since the volumes were very low. The company reports its Embedded Value (EV) on a market-consistent basis. You are the Chief Actuary of the company.
Q1i.question: Write down the heads under which the analysis of EV movement should be presented. [Max 5]

EV movement analysis heads: Plus: Expected return on free surplus
Plus: Return on in-force business — comprising (a) Expected return, (b) Experience variances, (c) Operating assumption changes
Plus: New business contribution; Less: Development costs = Operating return before tax and exceptional items
Plus: Investment return variances; Plus: Effect of economic assumption changes; Plus: Exceptional items = Return on EV before tax
Less: Attributed tax = Return on EV after tax
Plus: Capital raised; Less: Capital distributed = Closing Embedded Value

Q1ii.scenario: Same as Q1(i). You are explaining to the Board the detail behind each head of the EV movement analysis.
Q1ii.question: Provide details of each of the following EV movement heads: (a) Expected return on free surplus; (b) Return on in-force — expected return; (c) Return on in-force — experience variances; (d) Return on in-force — operating assumption changes; (e) New business contribution; (f) Investment return variances; (g) Effect of economic assumption changes. [Max 10]

a) Expected return on free surplus: The risk-free investment return assumption at the start of the year is applied to the free surplus at the start of the year.
The free surplus is assets less the Statutory reserve less the SCR.
b) Return on in-force — expected return: This is the risk-free rate applied to the opening VIF. It is sometimes called the 'unwind of the discount rate'.
It represents the value of the expected future profits being closer in time at the end of the analysis period, and thus being discounted by less and so having a higher value.
c) Return on in-force — experience varia: The basic approach is to roll forward the assets backing reserve to the end of the year using the beginning-of-year best estimate assumptions as the expected experience. As this roll forward is performed using best estimates, the assets and the BEL should still be equal at the end of the year.

Repeat the roll forward process but change one item of experience from the expected value to its actual value. The change in surplus at the end of the year gives the contribution from that item. Repeat this process changing each item of experience in turn to identify its contribution. Under this approach the experience variance from each source depends on the order in which each source is analysed.

The company will also need to consider the impact of experience variances on the other components of the EV. If actual experience is different from expected, then the VIF, RM and SCR components of the EV will also change.
So, an experience variance item will also be required for any difference in the actual releases of profit and capital to those expected.
d) Return on in-force — operating assump: These will emerge if the best estimate assumptions have changed. There will be separate operating assumption changes to consider for the EV experience (projection) basis.
Assumptions underlying the risk margin or capital requirement calculations might also change (e.g. correlation coefficients), which could also impact the EV.
The assumed 'cost of capital' factor might also change (i.e. the parameters used to determine the cost of holding the RM and SCR), the impact of which would also need to be analysed.
e) New business contribution: This is calculated as an increase in VIF plus an increase in net assets at the point of sale (assuming the new business is written on terms that are expected to be profitable).
This could be assessed by simply taking the difference between the EV with and without the inclusion of model points that relate to new business written over the year.
f) Investment return variances: In respect of the own funds, this item will include the difference in the change in assets backing the own funds over and above the assumed risk-free returns.

Another component will be the impact of the assets moving differently to the movement in BEL. The actual return must be analysed against the risk-free rate plus any matching adjustment or volatility adjustment.

There may also be an impact on the cost of holding the risk margin and additional capital — for example, the capital requirements may change due to actual investment returns being different from expected. There could also be a contribution if the risk-free yield curve moves differently to the asset yields.
Investment return variance on the VIF will reflect the actual investment return differing from the discount rate being used to discount the future profits.
g) Effect of economic assumption changes: This will be determined in a very similar way to the investment return variance but will consider the impact of changing the assumptions for future economic experience (whereas the investment return variance considered the impact of having different economic experience than expected last year).

It may be that some of these changes are combined with investment return variances, due to the close relationship between actual economic outcome over the year and the best estimate future economic assumptions, particularly in relation to yields and bond value movements.

Q1iii.scenario: Same as Q1(i). The company plans to focus its marketing campaign specifically on IP sales.
Q1iii.question: How will this focus on IP marketing impact the EV and each of the heads in the analysis of change in EV? [Max 10]

Impact on EV and analysis heads: There will only be any impact if the company's expectation that the focus on IP sales will significantly boost sales is borne out in reality.
There will be no impact on the current EV as that does not take account of new business.

Assuming that the business is normally written to be profitable on a best estimate basis, the future EV would be higher than otherwise. This will increase the new business contribution in the analysis of change in EV — although the effect of the marketing campaign specifically would not be separately identified.

An increase in sales will not directly affect in-force business experience. However, if future new business is expected to increase then the company may reduce the per-policy expense assumptions. This would increase the EV and would come through in the analysis as an operating assumption change.
It is unlikely that there will be a material impact on the experience variances, although a small expense surplus may occur due to higher policy numbers to cover fixed expenses.

It is possible that the future mix of business will change — for example, the average policy size and the demographic mix may change — but it will take some while before the full impact on the target market will be known.

The cost of the marketing campaign will be included in the EV. The marketing campaign will cost money, pushing down assets which will show up in the EV. This may be treated as an exceptional item or a development cost in the analysis.

The company may have to spend on systems and staff to deal with the increased business volumes. This spending would also reduce the EV and would partially offset the expense surplus items described above.
Since the selling of impaired life annuity is stopped already, it will not impact the current and projected future EV.

Q1iv.scenario: Same as Q1(i). EV Insurance is considering selling its IP book of business to another insurer. The CFO has asked whether the sale price is likely to be higher or lower than the published EV.
Q1iv.question: Explain whether you would expect the sale price to be higher or lower than the published EV. [Max 10]

Sale price vs published EV: The first thing to consider is the assets allocated in respect of the business concerned. If the two calculations were based on different assets, the values would be different for this reason alone (e.g. if the EV were based on 100m assets and 105m were transferred to the new insurer, this fact alone would lead to a payment bigger than the EV by 5m).

Consider how the sale price will be negotiated. It is likely that the purchaser and seller will both agree to use EV calculations to determine how much should be paid — but if the sale price is agreed by some other means, it is not possible to comment on its likely size in comparison with the EV.

If the EV is to be used, there will be a need to consider the basis for the calculations. The sale basis is a question of negotiation — the purchaser (seller) will favour a basis that produces a lower (higher) EV.
The sale of the business is not a 'forced' sale. The seller is therefore not in a weak position and may be able to achieve a reasonable price. Following this logic, the sale price may exceed the EV.

Conversely, if the withdrawal from the market has a specific driver (e.g. changing capital requirements of this class of business under Solvency II), the seller may be in a weaker position and not achieve as good a price.
The published EV is market-consistent so should in principle give values achievable when selling a block of business.

The purchaser may base the negotiations on their own expense levels rather than the Seller's. So, if the sale is achieved to a very low expense company, a price higher than the EV may be achieved because of the lower expenses expected to be incurred by the purchasing company. It is likely that any such synergy would be shared.
On the other hand, the sale price will need to allow for transaction costs and the costs of the new company taking the business on board.

It may be argued in negotiations that the event of selling the business will cause a surge of lapses/surrenders. The purchasing company may insist on a very cautious (i.e. big) allowance for lapses in the sale price. This might make the sale price lower than the published EV.

There may be an argument from the purchaser that there will be greater uncertainty regarding the experience of the business post-sale. The purchaser may therefore push for greater allowances for risk margins and required capital, resulting in a lower EV.

Another issue is that the published EV will be based on assumptions set at a single point in time each year. The sale price is a one-off that may be determined between annual reporting dates. The sale price may therefore be higher or lower because of unusual current conditions (e.g. unusually high or low interest rates).
Overall, with factors going in different directions, it is not possible to say which of the figures will be larger.

Q1v.scenario: Same as Q1(i). The Board has asked for an explanation of the difference between EV movement analysis and surplus analysis.
Q1v.question: Explain the difference between the EV movement analysis and the analysis of surplus. [Max 5]

EV movement vs surplus analysis: The EV movement analysis shows the net impact of deviation in current surplus and future profitability, whereas the surplus analysis shows the impact of deviation on current profits only.
A single head might have opposite impacts on EV and surplus. For example, an increase in lapse rates might have a positive impact on surplus but a negative impact on EV.

The surplus analysis will show the financial effect of divergences between actual experience and that assumed in the valuation basis. This will help with a review of the valuation assumptions, exposing which assumptions are the most financially significant. It must be remembered though, that valuation assumptions are long-term and should not be changed hastily in the light of just one year's results.

Generally, it will show the financial effect of writing new business. If the analysis is carried out independently of the valuation data, it can provide a check on both the valuation data and the valuation process.
It will help to identify any causes for concern, so that corrective action may be taken. The trends in the items of surplus may give useful information on trends in the experience of the company.

Q1vi.scenario: Same as Q1(i). EV Insurance also has a PMI portfolio. The Board has asked how the analysis of surplus for PMI differs from that for long-term insurance.
Q1vi.question: Explain how the analysis of surplus for the PMI product would differ from that for a long-term insurer. Outline the key items to monitor in the PMI analysis of surplus. [Max 10]

PMI vs long-term insurer analysis: For long-term insurances, the analysis of surplus looks at the surplus arising each year throughout their lifetime. For existing business at least, there may be little chance to react to adverse experience since the premium rates are usually fixed.
For PMI, there is the opportunity at renewal to change prices, product design, underwriting or claim conditions — and hence it is useful to analyse profit more regularly throughout the year.

An analysis of surplus would normally be an annual exercise for a long-term insurer. For a PMI insurer, the company would again assess the profitability of business by yearly cohort, but would also perform quarterly analyses to establish up-to-date positions.
The quarterly calculations will be fed through to the price being charged for new business, to take account of the most recent changes in claim trends.
Key items to monitor in PMI: Rates of incidence of claim — the number of separate claims divided by the exposed to risk.
Average cost per claim — the total incurred cost of claims divided by the number of separate claims.
Loss ratio — the total incurred cost of claims divided by the relevant earned premium.
In addition to these, analysis of persistency is also important, to assess the profits lost through withdrawals. Analysis of investment profit is not so important for the short-term business.
Whilst the loss ratio is quick and simple to calculate, any trends in either frequency or size of claim will be hidden. Therefore, if data allows, it is more useful to monitor the first two indices.
Each will be calculated for the cohorts of business under review and will be compared with the assumptions made in the original premium pricing.

Cohorts by duration are often reviewed since many PMI portfolios exhibit a selection effect — the loss ratio typically increases by duration, depending on the method and depth of medical underwriting at outset.
Incurred claims will include allowance for the total ultimate expected claim cost and so must include all claims paid to date (whether settled or not), and all claims yet to be reported.
The definition of a claim should be consistent so that trends in experience can be meaningfully analysed, and results compared to those of competitors.

If no claims discount systems are in operation, it is crucial to be able to assess experience by cohort accurately and quickly, in order to fine-tune the methodology of bonus-malus and promotion/demotion scales in operation.

Q2i.scenario: MedAll is a medium-sized standalone health insurance company. It is currently writing three lines of business: Individual Income Protection (IP), Group Medical Reimbursement (GMR) and Group Critical Illness (CI). Recently, the solvency ratio of MedAll has fallen to a borderline level.
Q2i.question: Briefly explain what is meant by solvency ratio and its drivers. [½ per point; Max 5]

Solvency ratio and its drivers: Solvency ratio refers to the ratio of available capital to required capital. Regulators require insurers to always maintain a certain level of solvency ratio.
Solvency ratio of <100% implies insolvency. Typically there is a threshold solvency ratio, say 120% — when solvency ratio tips below that level, the regulator starts to intervene.

Both available capital and required capital are determined based on the regulatory capital regime applicable. Required capital tends to reflect the risk profile — higher the risk, greater the capital required.

Risk profile includes biometric risks (mortality/morbidity/longevity) as well as others including: investment risk (both in absolute terms and in relation to the extent of asset/liability mismatch); operational risk (fraud, data loss, people, system, and other operational failures); counterparty risk (failure of third parties).
There is also typically a minimum capital requirement for doing insurance business. So the required capital is then Max(minimum capital, required capital).

Available capital is the excess of assets over the liabilities. Assets here refer to the value of assets to the extent admissible for regulatory solvency purposes. Liabilities are the value of policy obligations calculated as per regulatory guidance/rules.

Q2ii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). MedAll's solvency ratio has fallen to a borderline level.
Q2ii.question: Outline the implications of such a solvency ratio to the business of the company. [½ per point; Max 5]

Implications of borderline solvency: It is likely that regulatory intervention will kick in — that might include limiting new business/closure to new business, and immediate requirement to pump in capital by the shareholders.
Bad publicity leading to lower sales. Brokers not promoting the company products leading to lower sales. Lower sales leading to poor contribution to company overheads, worsening the financials.
If the company is traded, drop in share price. Any company expansion plans will likely be stalled.
Potential impact on salary increase/bonus payout to staff. Likely increase in staff turnover and salesforce turnover. Likely impact on staff morale affecting customer service quality.
Increased lapse/surrender, again worsening the financials.

Q2iii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The Board of MedAll has asked the Chief Actuary to investigate the causes of and remedies for the borderline solvency position.
Q2iii.question: Outline the areas the Chief Actuary should investigate to understand the causes and identify possible actions to address the low solvency. [½ per point; Max 9]

Investigation areas: Look at the drivers of solvency (available capital and required capital) from both asset and liability sides.

Any asset admissibility issues due to domiciling of assets, type of assets etc.? Can they be addressed by reallocation of assets? Need to consider cost implications of sale/purchase that might be involved.
Reserving assumptions more prudent than required by regulations?
Claims outgo larger than expected (large claims/catastrophe/pandemic etc.) — one-off or recurring?

Could the results have been better with a different reinsurance structure? Cost/benefit implications of such a reinsurance? Impact of different reinsurance structures on the required capital. While more reinsurance might help reduce capital requirement, it might increase the counterparty risk and hence required capital.
Profit testing of the products — are we charging enough? Expense analysis — are there areas to get more efficient? Would outsourcing of some of the activities be more efficient?

Shareholder's appetite to invest more — especially if the solvency challenge is driven by large and sudden growth in volumes, or caused by change in accounting rules/practices and it is a transition issue, or a new capital-intensive product sold a lot.
Customer persistency analysis — is higher or lower than expected withdrawals causing any strain? Analysis of new business strain — would change of sales incentive structures help?

Impact of financial reinsurance arrangement (if permitted by the regulations). Analysis of ALM position and solvency implications. Any operational issues — fraud, waste etc. and how they can be fixed.

Q2iv.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The regulator has asked MedAll to submit a recovery plan outlining how it intends to restore its solvency position.
Q2iv.question: Outline what should be covered in the recovery plan submitted to the regulator. [½ per point; Max 6]

Recovery plan contents: Summary of analyses done, outlining the key causes of the solvency drop and whether the issue is systemic or temporary.
Business plan for the next few years and its impact on solvency.
List of actions that will be taken with rationale for each, the impact of them on the solvency, and timelines for execution of each action item.
Projected solvency position over the next few years as per regulatory solvency rules, certified by the Appointed Actuary.
Regular monitoring and internal governance/regulatory reporting.
Implications of the borderline solvency on PRE (policyholder reasonable expectations) and how they would be addressed — how will customer/investor confidence be maintained/restored.
Whether the plan would be made public and why. Why no regulatory action is necessary.

Q2v.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The CFO of MedAll has suggested that expense management is not a priority since it is not related to solvency.
Q2v.question: Explain why the CFO is wrong and why expense management is relevant to solvency. [½ per point; Max 3]

Why expenses matter for solvency: Expense is an item of outgo for the insurer, and expense overrun could be eating into available capital. This is typically a challenge for small/medium-size insurers, especially in early years when there are no economies of scale.
Risk-based capital frameworks include expense risk (including expense inflation) as a driver of required capital.
Even otherwise, expense management is a key element for prudent management of an insurer and a driver of profitability and hence capital.

Q2vi.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The Chief Actuary is conducting a detailed expense analysis for MedAll.
Q2vi.question: Describe the five categories of expenses and the unit measurements for each. [1½ per expense type; Max 6]

Five expense categories: Acquisition expenses: relate to sales and marketing, underwriting (including expenses related to persistency management). Unit measurements include no. of applications, no. of policies issued, face amount.

Administration expenses: relate to the ongoing policy-servicing costs (including premium collections, operational management, monitoring and reporting). Unit measurements include no. of policies in-force, premium income, no. of transactions.

Claim expenses: relate to validating and paying claims (including any legal costs associated with disputed claims). Unit measurements include no. of claims, size of claim, no. of cancellation/refund processed.
Investment expenses: relate to managing the insurer's investment portfolio. Unit measurements include market value, acquired value, sale value.

Overhead expenses: other costs not included elsewhere, including executive management, actuarial, legal, HR, IT, etc. Unit measurements include required capital, no. of employees, premium volume, percentage of non-overhead expenses.

Q2vii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The Chief Actuary is now conducting a product-level expense analysis for each of MedAll's three products.
Q2vii.question: Describe how you would conduct the expense analysis for each of the following products and what you would monitor: (a) Individual Income Protection; (b) Group Medical Reimbursement; (c) Group Critical Illness. [½ per point; Max 3 each]

a) Individual Income Protection: Long-term product — initial expenses involve intermediary commission, underwriting and setting up policy in the admin systems. Commission and admin are mostly directly linked to policies issued (no. and size); overheads linked to volume, so check if sales are in line with expected.

Cost of underwriting is driven by applications and needs to be spread over the policies — so, track the placement ratio (applications to policies issued). Lower placement than expected means higher expenses than expected.

Multiple claims on the same policy — so, incidence as well as termination rates drive the expenses associated. Monitor if actual claims (incidence as well as termination) are in line with expected — deviation could point to an expense gap.
Expense inflation is a key item for this product — check if the actual inflation is in line with that allowed for in the pricing.
b) Group Medical Reimbursement: Short-term product — group and so less underwriting — initial expense is largely commission and policy setup. These vary between new business and renewals, so sales and persistency will drive the spread of overheads.

Significant claim expenses linked to number and size — will depend upon in-house vs outsourced to a TPA. In-house: higher volume/complexity of claims and lower business volume would invalidate the expense assumption — so check actual to expected.
Similarly, TPA fee linked to number of members/volumes will depart from pricing if the numbers differ. Expense allowance for other overheads will again be linked to volume.
Expense inflation is less of an issue with this being a short-term product.
c) Group Critical Illness: Short-term product — initial expense is largely commission and policy setup. Key is to check if volume is sufficient to cover fixed overheads.
Some underwriting will be involved if this is voluntary — so, level of participation is important to monitor. Lower than expected participation means lower contribution to expenses than expected.

Less likely to be stand-alone and typically attached to group life cover — so, the rider take-up will drive this benefit's contribution to the full product. Check actual to expected take-up — the lower the take-up, the lower the contribution to expenses.
Claim expense linked to number (and to some extent size) of claims — so monitor claim experience. Higher than expected incidence of claims means higher than expected expenses.

Q2viii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The Board of MedAll is considering financial reinsurance (FinRe) as a solution to its solvency problem.
Q2viii.question: Discuss the suitability of financial reinsurance as a solution to MedAll's solvency problem. [½ per point; Max 7]

Financial reinsurance suitability: Suitability will depend upon the nature of the problem that the insurer is facing — what drives the low solvency. Also, whether or not the solution is acceptable to the regulator, and the cost vs benefit of the solution.

Lump sum fund in return for profit from reinsurance: assets created by advancing of funds to the insurer by the reinsurer, with the repayment contingent upon surplus from reinsurance in future years — so no liability created, thereby improving assets over liabilities.

Value of in-force (VIF) monetisation: recognising profit embedded in the in-force blocks through reinsurance. Works when you have large blocks of profitable long-term insurance business — which is less likely in this situation for a medium-size health insurer.
Commission financing: helps reduce new business strain. Again, works for long-term business with significant initial commission/underwriting and other acquisition costs.
Assets restructuring: through which attempts are made to reduce the inadmissibility of assets, for example due to asset domiciling requirements of the regulator.
Overall, given the nature of this case (medium-size health insurer), it is less likely that FinRe solutions might help here.
